title: Sortino downside deviation mixes two different downside thresholds

evidence.quote:
pm := NewPerformanceMetrics()
	pm.Calculate(trades, equityCurve, decimal.New(10000), decimal.New(10150), 252)

	t.Logf("Sortino Ratio: %v", pm.SortinoRatio)

reasoning: The numerator sums squared deviations for trades below the sample mean, but the denominator counts trades below zero. When those sets differ, variance is divided by a count that does not match the observations included in the sum, and it may return the 0.0001 fallback even though sumSquares contains below-mean observations. This materially misstates SortinoRatio for common positive-return samples.
recommendation: Use one threshold consistently. Prefer a target/minimum acceptable return for both selecting downside observations and computing deviations, or consistently use mean if that is the intended contract.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: The Sortino performance test only logs the ratio and uses data where the below-mean losing trades are also below zero, so the mismatched-count path is not asserted.
suggestedRegressionTest: Use trades with positive returns below the mean but no negative returns, and assert the downside deviation/Sortino behavior matches the chosen target-return contract.
